- **T…ôhl√ľk…ôl…ôr**: S…ôtirl…ôrd…ô s…ôhv indeksl…ôm…ô (`ArgumentOutOfRangeException`) v…ô ya √ßoxsaylńĪ birl…ôŇüdirm…ô …ôm…ôliyyatlarńĪ performansńĪ aŇüańüńĪ sala bil…ôr. `null` s…ôtirl…ôrl…ô iŇül…ôm…ôk x…ôtalara s…ôb…ôb ola bil…ôr.

KodlarńĪ Visual Studio Code-da .NET SDK il…ô sńĪnaqdan ke√ßirin (`dotnet run`). Microsoft-un r…ôsmi s…ôn…ôdl…ôrind…ôn (https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/csharp/) istifad…ô edin.

Bu kodda:

In C# programming, strings (`string`) are used to store and manipulate text data. A string is a sequence of characters (e.g., letters, numbers, symbols) and is immutable, meaning its content cannot be changed after creation, though new strings can be created. This JSON file explains the definition, creation, and common methods of strings in a simple way. Code examples are compatible with .NET 8, but due to limited C# support in Pyodide, they should be tested with Visual Studio Code or Visual Studio using .NET SDK. Run the code with the `dotnet run` command. Examples use static values and do not require user input.
Strings are created using double quotes (`"`) and can be used for various operations.

In this code:
- `string message` holds a string value.
- `message.Length` returns the number of characters in the string.
- Note: Run this code with .NET SDK in Visual Studio Code using `dotnet run`.
Strings can be concatenated using the `+` operator or the `string.Concat` method.

In this code:
- The `+` operator concatenates `firstName` and `lastName`.
- `string.Concat` serves the same purpose.
- Results are printed to the console.
Strings can be formatted using `string.Format` or string interpolation (`$`).

In this code:
- `string.Format` inserts variables into a string using indices.
- String interpolation (`$`) is a simpler and more readable approach.
- Both methods produce the same result.
C# provides many built-in methods for working with strings.

In this code:
- `ToUpper()` converts the string to uppercase.
- `ToLower()` converts the string to lowercase.
- `Length` returns the number of characters.
- `Substring(0, 5)` extracts a portion of the string (from index 0 to 5).
- `Replace("World", "C#")` replaces one substring with another.
- `StartsWith("Hello")` checks if the string starts with a specific substring.
- `string.IsNullOrEmpty(text)` checks if the string is empty or null.
Strings simplify the handling of text data.

items.description: Flexibility: Various methods make it easy to manipulate text.
Readability: String interpolation makes code more readable.
UTF-8 Support: C# strings support international characters.

content.text: Working with strings can present challenges for beginners.

items.description: Immutability: Strings are immutable, creating new strings for each operation, which can increase memory usage.
Invalid Indexing: Methods like `Substring` can cause errors with incorrect indices.
Pyodide Limitation: C# code is not fully supported in Pyodide.

content.text:
- **Dangers**: Incorrect indexing (`ArgumentOutOfRangeException`) or excessive concatenations can degrade performance. Working with `null` strings can cause errors.
- **Recommendations**: Carefully check indices and use `StringBuilder` for multiple concatenations. Use `string.IsNullOrEmpty` or `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace` for null checks. Test code with .NET SDK in Visual Studio Code (`dotnet run`). Refer to Microsoft‚Äôs official documentation (https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/csharp/).
This example causes an error due to invalid indexing.

In this code:
- `text.Substring(10, 5)` tries to access an index beyond the string‚Äôs length.
- The string `Hello` has a length of 5, but index 10 does not exist.
- Solution: Ensure indices are within bounds using `text.Length`.
